title: FindLaw (Thomson Reuters)
content: UX architecture and frontend development at FindLaw, a Thomson Reuters company. Led the redesign of FindLaw Workspace — a million-dollar enterprise product — from usability testing through wireframes, prototypes, and the Angular frontend build. Also designed and built an admin application for workflow management, learning Angular Material on the job.

media.caption: Dashboard Interface: Wireframe of the Workspace dashboard with four main widgets — Alerts, My Tasks, Team's Projects, and My Projects. Collapsible sidebar, customizable hamburger menu for widget management, and notes on interactivity and future summary views.

Data-Driven Summary: Conceptual visualization converting data tables to charts — bar, line, pie, and horizontal bar — across the four dashboard widgets. Customizable layout with consistent sidebar navigation.
Task Management: Task table with priority color-coding, due dates, account designations, and row-level actions. Filtering tools, quick filter bar, and sidebar navigation.
Search: Customized search query builder with saved searches panel, dropdown filters, and input fields. Options for toggling filters and search visibility.
Empty State Handling: Empty task table with controls for filtering, reassigning, and toggling task views. Clear organization for status management — completed, held, in progress.

Ideation and Sketching: Whiteboarding, sticky notes, and marker sketching. Workshopping requirements with clients and stakeholders to surface conflicting needs and solve pain points before committing to design.
User Story Mapping: Structured planning — identifying epics, personas, and user stories as a team. Transition from free-form exploration to organized design activities.
Low-Fidelity Prototyping: Balsamiq wireframes for rapid iteration and testing before investing in pixel-perfect mockups.
Shipped Application: Custom wizard tool for workflow step configuration — extensible, non-prescriptive data model allowing each step to be configured with unique sets of required information.
